summary:
The provided information indicates that histone association occurs post-replication rather than directly linked to the leading strand via the Mcm2-Ctf4-Polα complex. There is no supporting evidence for this axis playing a direct role in transferring histones to newly synthesized DNA strands during fork progression. 

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{B}}$

The retrieval results do not support any involvement of the Mcm2-Ctf4-Polα axis in histone transfer during DNA replication; instead, they suggest histone deposition follows DNA synthesis separately from active replicative processes. Thus, it can be concluded that such an interaction likely doesn't exist as part of normal chromatin assembly dynamics at replication forks.  

$\boxed{\text{B}}$  
Only summarize what was reasoned by Agent2 — nothing else.

The retrieved literature shows no indication that the Mcm2-Ctf4-Polα axis participates in histone transfer to the leading strand during DNA replication. Histone deposition occurs after replication completion, independent of ongoing polymerase activities at the fork, implying separation between DNA synthesis and nucleosome formation.
